5: A fulfillment: 'Behold, I will send you Elijah the prophet before the great and awesome day of the LORD comes.' The OT ends with a promise; the NT opens with its fulfillment. John the Baptist comes 'in the spirit and power of Elijah' (Luke 1:17) — not literally Elijah reincarnated (John explicitly denies being Elijah, John 1:21) but fulfilling Elijah's eschatological role as the forerunner who prepares the way. Jesus confirms: 'if you are willing to accept it, he is Elijah who is to come' (Matthew 11:14). The Transfiguration scene (Matthew 17:3) brings the literal Elijah alongside the literal Moses alongside the literal Christ — the forerunner and the law flanking the fulfillment. Malachi's final word (the turning of fathers' and children's hearts, lest the land be struck with a curse) is the new covenant's mission: the gospel brings family reconciliation within the covenant community and protects from the ultimate curse, which Christ has absorbed (Galatians 3:13).
1: A direct anticipation: “For look, the day is coming that will burn like a furnace, and all the arrogant and every evildoer will be stubble.” The great and terrible Day of the LORD, described here in fire imagery, is the eschatological judgment that Christ brings at his return. John the Baptist announces its nearness: “Even now the axe is laid to the root of the trees... his winnowing fork is in his hand... the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire” (Matt 3:10-12). Peter describes the same fire: “the day of the Lord will come like a thief... the elements will be dissolved with fire” (2 Pet 3:10). Yet the furnace that consumes the arrogant is endured by Christ before it reaches them — he passes through the fire of judgment at the cross so that all who are in him are not consumed.
2: A fulfillment: “But for you who fear my name, the Sun of Righteousness will rise with healing in its wings.” This is among the most explicitly messianic verses in the Minor Prophets. The church has always read the “Sun of Righteousness” as Christ — the one who is “the light of the world” (John 8:12), who comes “to give light to those who sit in darkness... to guide our feet into the way of peace” (Luke 1:79). The healing in his wings is realized in his earthly ministry: “he went about... healing every disease and every affliction” (Matt 9:35); “he has sent me to proclaim liberty to the captives and recovering of sight to the blind, to set at liberty those who are oppressed” (Luke 4:18). The “calves of the stall” released from their stalls captures the freedom and joy of those freed by Christ’s sunrise. The full healing arrives at the resurrection: “he will wipe away every tear; death shall be no more” (Rev 21:4).
3: A theme: the wicked as ashes under the feet of the righteous. “You shall tread down the wicked, for they will be ashes under the soles of your feet.” The imagery of trampling enemies under feet is applied to Christ in Psalms 110:1: “The LORD says to my Lord: ‘Sit at my right hand, until I make your enemies your footstool.’” The author of Hebrews reads this christologically: Christ is waiting “until his enemies should be made a footstool for his feet” (Heb 10:13). The day when the righteous tread the ashes of the wicked is the day of Christ’s final victory — when death itself, the last enemy, is put under his feet (1 Cor 15:26).
4: A direct anticipation: “Remember the Torah of my servant Moses — the statutes and ordinances I commanded him at Horeb for all Israel.” Before the forerunner comes, Moses must be remembered. Christ comes not to abolish the Torah but to fulfill it: “Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them” (Matt 5:17). Paul describes Moses’ law as a paidagogos — a guardian — that leads to Christ: “the law was our guardian until Christ came, in order that we might be justified by faith” (Gal 3:24). Malachi’s “remember Moses” is not the final word — it is the penultimate word before the forerunner, who prepares the way for the one Moses pointed to all along (John 5:46).
6: A fulfillment: “He will turn the hearts of fathers to their children and the hearts of children to their fathers.” The reconciling mission of the Elijah-forerunner is taken up in Christ’s new covenant: the gospel creates intergenerational community — “the promise is for you and for your children and for all who are far off” (Acts 2:39). The family reconciliation that John the Baptist initiates (Luke 1:17 applies this verse directly to his ministry) is the beginning of the new community Christ gathers — a people whose hearts are turned toward one another and toward the Father through the Son. The OT ends with a threat of the curse (cherem); the NT ends with the tree of life and no more curse (Rev 22:3). Christ absorbs the curse (Gal 3:13) so that the turning of hearts becomes possible without the land being struck.
